longDescription:
Jam-e Jam - The World-Reflecting Goblet of Persian Legend The Cup of Jamshid (Jam-e Jam) is one of the most celebrated magical objects in Persian
                    mythology and literature. This legendary goblet, filled with the elixir of immortality,
                    possessed the miraculous power to reflect the entire world within its depths. By gazing
                    into the cup, its possessor could see everything happening anywhere in the seven regions
                    of the earth.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi
sources.description: The definitive epic poem of Persian literature, recounting the history and mythology of Iran, including detailed accounts of Jamshid and his Cup.
sources.title: Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi

sources.name: The History of al-Tabari (Tarikh al-Tabari)
sources.description: A comprehensive historical chronicle by the Persian scholar Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari, providing historical context and alternative perspectives on the legends surrounding Jamshid.
sources.title: The History of al-Tabari (Tarikh al-Tabari)

sources.name: Yasht 9 (Zam-Yast)
sources.description: A hymn from the Avesta, the sacred texts of Zoroastrianism, that references figures and concepts related to Jamshid and his reign, providing insights into the pre-Islamic origins of the mythology.
sources.title: Yasht 9 (Zam-Yast)

sources.name: Bundahishn
sources.description: A Zoroastrian text explaining the creation of the world and early history, offering cosmological background relevant to understanding the significance of Jamshid's era.
sources.title: Bundahishn

sources.name: Persian Myths by Vesta Sarkhosh Curtis
sources.description: A modern academic work that explores various aspects of Persian mythology, including the significance of Jamshid and his legendary goblet.
sources.title: Persian Myths by Vesta Sarkhosh Curtis

powers: Reveals all the world and its secrets
shortDescription: Jam-e Jam - The World-Reflecting Goblet of Persian Legend
name: Cup of Jamshid

extendedContent.content:
The Cup of Jamshid (Jam-e Jam) is one of the most celebrated magical objects in Persian
                    mythology and literature. This legendary goblet, filled with the elixir of immortality,
                    possessed the miraculous power to reflect the entire world within its depths. By gazing
                    into the cup, its possessor could see everything happening anywhere in the seven regions
                    of the earth.
                
                
                    The cup belonged to the legendary King Jamshid, one of the greatest rulers of the mythical
                    Pishdadian dynasty. In Jamshid's reign, humanity lived in a golden age of prosperity and
                    peace, and the cup represented the king's divine wisdom and far-seeing judgment.
extendedContent.title: Description and Nature

extendedContent.content:
King Jamshid
                
                    Jamshid was the fourth king of the world in Persian mythology, ruling during humanity's
                    golden age. Under his reign, which lasted 700 years, there was no disease, death, or
                    suffering. He is credited with discovering medicine, dividing society into classes,
                    teaching metalworking, and establishing Nowruz (the Persian New Year).
                
                
                    However, Jamshid's downfall came through pride. After centuries of glorious rule, he
                    began to believe his achievements were his own rather than gifts from Ahura Mazda
                    (the supreme god). His divine farr (kingly glory) departed, and he was eventually
                    overthrown and killed by the serpent-shouldered tyrant Zahhak.
                

                
                    "The cup of Jamshid wherein the world was reflected - seek it in the ruins of the heart."
                    — Hafez, Persian Sufi poet
                

                Sufi Interpretation
                
                    In Sufi poetry, the Cup of Jamshid became a powerful mystical symbol. The cup represents
                    the heart of the spiritual seeker, which when purified becomes a mirror reflecting
                    divine truth. The world-revealing power of the cup symbolizes enlightenment and the
                    direct perception of reality that comes through spiritual discipline.
                
                
                    Poets like Hafez and Rumi used the Cup of Jamshid as a metaphor for the soul's journey
                    toward God, suggesting that the true cup is not an external object but the polished
                    heart of the mystic who has transcended ego and worldly attachments.
extendedContent.title: Mythology and Legend

extendedContent.content:
The Cup of Jamshid holds immense importance in Persian cultural identity. It appears
                    throughout the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi, the national epic of Iran, and
                    pervades Persian poetry from the medieval period to the present day.
                
                
                    The image of the world-reflecting cup has been compared to similar objects in other
                    traditions: the Holy Grail, the mirror of Galadriel, and various scrying devices. Some
                    scholars have suggested connections between Persian and Celtic vessel mythology, though
                    direct influence is difficult to establish.
extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance

symbolism:
The Cup of Jamshid holds immense importance in Persian cultural identity. It appears
                    throughout the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi, the national epic of Iran, and
                    pervades Persian poetry from the medieval period to the present day.
                
                
                    The image of the world-reflecting cup has been compared to similar objects in other
                    traditions: the Holy Grail, the mirror of Galadriel, and various scrying devices. Some
                    scholars have suggested connections between Persian and Celtic vessel mythology, though
                    direct influence is difficult to establish.

keyMyths.title: Jamshid's Golden Age
keyMyths.description: King Jamshid, using the Cup, ruled over a golden age of prosperity and immortality. The Cup allowed him to see the state of his kingdom and the world, ensuring justice and preventing hardship, thus ushering in an era free of death and disease.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Jamshid
keyMyths.description: Jamshid's arrogance, fueled by his power and the knowledge gained from the Cup, led him to declare himself a god. This hubris angered Ahura Mazda and ultimately caused his downfall, as the divine favor was withdrawn and his kingdom fell into chaos, losing him the Cup's blessing.

keyMyths.title: Divination and Prophecy
keyMyths.description: The Cup of Jamshid was not merely a drinking vessel but a powerful tool for divination. By gazing into its depths, Jamshid could foresee future events, understand the motivations of others, and receive guidance, making him an unparalleled ruler and seer.

keyMyths.title: The Cup's Connection to the Divine
keyMyths.description: The origin of the Cup is often attributed to divine creation or gifting, linking it to Ahura Mazda or other celestial beings. Its power is derived from this divine connection, making it a sacred artifact capable of revealing hidden truths and influencing fate.

keyMyths.title: The Cup as a Symbol of Sovereignty
keyMyths.description: Possession of the Cup of Jamshid became synonymous with legitimate rule and divine right. Later rulers and heroes sought the Cup not only for its prophetic abilities but also as a symbol of their authority and claim to the throne, solidifying their power.
